# Time-to-event thinking, more broadly

## The same machinery runs through all of this

The serial interval is one censored, truncated, dependent delay.
The same survival-analysis ideas reappear right across infectious
disease modelling, often under other names.

| Survival analysis | Infectious disease modelling |
|---|---|
| Right truncation | Real-time observation; the nowcasting problem |
| Interval censoring | Daily or weekly aggregation of event times |
| Competing risks | Death vs recovery; ascertainment vs loss |
| Multi-state model | Compartmental S to E to I to R or D |
| Frailty / random effects | Individual heterogeneity; superspreading |
| Relative survival | Excess mortality over a baseline |

::: {.callout-note}
None of these is metaphor.
Each is the same likelihood, so the tools transfer in both directions.
:::

## A worked example: real-time Ebola outbreak size

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
The 2026 DRC Bundibugyo virus (Ebola) outbreak, with cases also in
Uganda.
Question: how big is it *now*, when most infections are not yet
reported?

- A joint Bayesian generative model (Turing.jl): a discrete-time
  renewal process fitting several streams in one posterior
- Streams: DRC suspected and confirmed cases and deaths, plus the
  cases and deaths exported to Uganda
- An ascertainment model for each surveillance system, so reported
  counts are read as a censored view of latent infections
- A no-onward-transmission projection of deaths already committed
  gives a lower bound on the outbreak's current size
- Refreshed in real time as each situation report arrives
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.callout-note}
## Same tools, scaled up
The delays use the same double-interval-censoring machinery from this
talk; the deaths-vs-recovery split is competing risks; recent counts
are right-truncated, so this is nowcasting.

[BVDOutbreakSize](https://github.com/epiforecasts/BVDOutbreakSize) ·
[live report](https://epiforecasts.io/BVDOutbreakSize/stable) ·
[DOI](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20312758)

Abbott, Sherratt, Brand, Funk. Compares with McCabe et al.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
Different from the practice section: that was the **2012 Isiro** line
list (estimating *delays*).
This is the **2026 DRC** outbreak (estimating *size*).
Complementary: the Isiro onset-to-death delays inform the priors here.
:::
:::
::::
